text: So I'll be sharing today a robotic tap incisional flank hernia. This lady is a fifty-five year old female who came to me after having a sigmoid colectomy and had an extraction site that had developed a flank hernia that you see here on the CT scan.

The surgeon docks the robotic system on the patient's right lateral side and uses cautery to remove small adhesions, revealing the extent of the hernia defect measuring approximately four by six centimeters at the semilunar line.
The surgeon observes omentum attached to the inferior portion of the hernia and decides to leave it in place, anticipating potential challenges in creating a full preperitoneal flap.

The surgeon begins creating a preperitoneal flap, with ports positioned just lateral to the midline. The right lower quadrant port is noted to be positioned closely, causing some difficulty during dissection.
The surgeon observes the hernia, which appears longer and more medial than initially anticipated, after completing the flap dissection.

Preoperatively, I was a little concerned about how low this hernia would be in relation to her colon. And so she was bumped a little bit in the left lateral decubitus, not full, just a slight towel under her abdomen.
The surgeon begins creating a flap by dissecting the thin peritoneum around the hernia sac, aiming to achieve the volcano sign before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum, revealing the attenuated transversalis fascia beneath it, which appears thinner than initially anticipated.

The surgeon adjusts the camera angle for improved visibility, revealing the hernia sacs becoming thin. Dissection is performed both cephalad and caudad to the hernia, aiming to encircle the structure completely.
The surgeon maintains dissection within the true preperitoneal plane, easily separating the retroperitoneal fat from the transversalis fascia laterally.

The surgeon identifies the hernia sac and creates a hole in the surrounding tissue. They attempt to recruit as much of the sac as possible to facilitate the placement of a non-coated mesh, ensuring full coverage over the mesh area.

The surgeon visualizes the lateral portion of the hernia, with the hernia sac coming into view. Despite encountering a few holes, the surgeon re-enters the preperitoneal plane laterally to continue the dissection.
The surgeon has completed the dissection around the hernia sac and is attempting to recruit as much of the sac as possible to prevent seroma formation.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection in the preperitoneal plane, removing retroperitoneal fat to achieve a five-centimeter overlap on all sides. The anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) is palpated, allowing for dissection five centimeters distal to the inferior border.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the caudal side, encountering difficulty due to the proximity of their right arm, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon observes the distinct layers of the abdominal wall, including the internal and external oblique muscles appearing together as a bulky anterior layer, and the transversalis muscle separately. The decision is made to close these layers separately.

The surgeon uses a permanent OV lock to close the internal and external oblique complex at an oblique angle, with the tissue layers visibly coming together easily. The pneumoperitoneum pressure is set to eight millimeters of mercury to aid in the closure.
The surgeon takes bites of the pseudo hernia sac, aiming to prevent seroma formation by securing the sac tissue.

The surgeon uses a six-inch permanent V-lock suture to close the transversalis layer, visible near the camera. The attenuated transversalis fascia is re-approximated to address the true defect, with plans to evaluate if additional sutures are needed.
The surgeon takes a large bite of the transversalis muscle, causing it to bulge and bleed slightly.

The surgeon flips the camera to reveal the attenuated transversalis fascia in a clearer view, showing a different trajectory from the previous suturing. An absorbable OV lock suture, longer than six inches, is selected for closure.

And this is where, you know, I think, question about what kind of overlap I need at that attenuated portion. You know, should I be four or five centimeters past that attenuated most medial edge? Probably. Again, my flap was... you know, a little bit too close to kind of get a true five centimeter overlap at that most medial portion.
The surgeon places a 15 by 15 cm circular, soft, macroporous Bard mesh over the complete defect, ensuring a five-centimeter overlap for secure coverage.
and I was very happy with how it looked in the end. So I'm going to try to get a little more room

The surgeon measures the surgical space, ensuring it is approximately seven centimeters. The measurement includes a four by four area encompassing all three layers, with an additional attenuated portion measuring three by two and a half centimeters.
The surgeon brings in a circular, macroporous mesh and lays it flat over the surgical site. The marked center of the mesh is aligned with the suture line to ensure proper placement without wrinkles.
The surgeon sutures the mesh into place using 2-0 Vicryl sutures, securing it at cardinal points with one suture in the center and four around the perimeter to prevent sliding.

I think one thing that's nice about this mesh is it's not a pro grip, so it doesn't really have any sticking features, but just with the little bit of blood and dissection and how macroporous, it actually sticks pretty nicely to the anterior abdominal wall. not flopping in your face. If you've used the Ventralite STs, you know that that coating and kind of the thickness of the mesh will make it pretty difficult to sit without someone holding it or putting sutures in place, which definitely can easily be done, but I really like how this mesh kind of will just stick and I don't have to worry about it.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture to close the surgical flap, with visible handling of the suture material. The omentum remains tacked to the inferior edge, aiding in the closure process.
The surgeon uses the omentum as a patch, providing additional tissue for reinforcement in the surgical area.

I'm only a couple years out from starting to do robotic cases on my own and I definitely have a lot of efficiencies to improve on in terms of suturing and holding the needle and things that are slowly getting better just with time and experiencing cases. So I know I have that big hole I'm about to close. This is probably the most challenging part just because I don't have another arm to hold up the flap. I kind of have to keep readjusting to see the hole and I just incorporate the momentum as part of that.

The surgeon inspects the surgical area for any remaining holes around the omentum and identifies one cranially. Using leftover Vicryl suture, the surgeon secures the mesh and closes the identified hole with a figure-eight stitch.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 suture to close a visible hole, ensuring no lateral holes are present due to the omental flap, to prevent potential herniation or mesh exposure.

But overall, I think the case went really well. Total time was about two and a half hours. She went home the same day. I have not yet seen her in follow up. But definitely a really interesting case. I just haven't seen a lot of spaghettians or I would say flank hernias where you can see the different layers of the abdominal wall so well and being able to kind of close both the anterior and posterior sheath I think gave me a really good repair. So thanks for watching. Feel free to critique and let me know what you think. Thanks.
